coredata.dc:title: Influence of ZnO: Ce3+/ Eu3+ doped and co-doped nanopowders on the properties of poly (ε-caprolactone) nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Malimabe, M.A.
Motloung, S.V.
Motaung, T.E.
Swart, H.C.
Dejene, F.B.
Koao, L.F.
coredata.dc:description: In this work the novel poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL)/ZnO: Ce3+, Eu3+ nanocomposites have been prepared through the solution casting method using acetophenone as solvent. The properties of the final composites were investigated by XRD, SEM, TGA, DSC, UV and PL. Diffraction patterns showed crystalline peaks at Bragg angles 2θ = 21.3° and 23.6° for PCL and the nanocomposite. Nanosphosphors are clearly dispersed and seem to have occupied PCL micro-cavities with expected elemental composition. The degree of crystallinity and overall thermal stability of the PCL nanocomposites are lower than of the pristine polymer. UV–vis transmittance spectra of the PCL showed absorption edge at lower wavelengths than all nanocomposites and nanopowders. There is an apparent improvement in the luminescent properties of the solution casted nanocomposites. In fact, the luminescence intensity of the co-doped nanocomposite samples has increased roughly10 times compared to PCL.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work the novel poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL)/ZnO: Ce3+, Eu3+ nanocomposites have been prepared through the solution casting method using acetophenone as solvent. The properties of the final composites were investigated by XRD, SEM, TGA, DSC, UV and PL. Diffraction patterns showed crystalline peaks at Bragg angles 2θ = 21.3° and 23.6° for PCL and the nanocomposite. Nanosphosphors are clearly dispersed and seem to have occupied PCL micro-cavities with expected elemental composition. The degree of crystallinity and overall thermal stability of the PCL nanocomposites are lower than of the pristine polymer. UV–vis transmittance spectra of the PCL showed absorption edge at lower wavelengths than all nanocomposites and nanopowders. There is an apparent improvement in the luminescent properties of the solution casted nanocomposites. In fact, the luminescence intensity of the co-doped nanocomposite samples has increased roughly10 times compared to PCL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Development of new materials for an alternative energy and bioengineering applications, in particular polymer/phosphor nanocomposites, has become a new interesting area of research during the last decade [1–3]. However, investigation and improvement of their physical properties are still a subject of research for optimization and development of their applications. For decades, environmental concerns and shortage of petroleum resources have driven researchers to bulk production of biodegradable and bio-based polymers such as Poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL)- and Poly (l-lactic acid) (PLA)-based phosphors, which presents a promising application in bioengineering [4–7].

PCL is amongst competent polymers due to its solubility in various solvent and semi–rigid at room temperature [2,8]. What makes it more attractive, especially in the medical uses, is the fact that it is biocompatible, somehow permeable and non-toxic to living organisms [9,10]. Its top promising applications include packaging (carrier bags, fruit and vegetables bags or plastic, and cheese compost bags) and agricultural sector (foils and plastic flowerpots). In addition, it can easily react with many other solvents or compounds. In fact, because of its soft and hard tissue that can be re-absorbed, it has been considered as potential substrate for wide range of applications [10,11]. Nonetheless, its low thermal and mechanical properties, compared to other commercially competent polymers, are disappointing for expansion of applications. Fortunately, the literature proposed that a preparation of PCL nanocomposite is one of the alternative methods to circumvent these drawbacks [12,13]. It is clear that its nanocomposites end up with different optical properties. For example, it has been found to have maximum UV–Vis–NIR absorption sensitivity around 280 nm and an energy band gap (Eg) of 4.4 eV [2,14–16]. Mallakpour et al. [16] evaluated the physiochemical properties of the PCL-based nanocomposites and identified two bands due to PCL at 260–290 and 190–220 nm wavelength regions. These bands correspond to the n→π* and π→π* transitions of the ester carbonyl, respectively [16].

Performance of the PCL can easily be improved through addition of small amount of inorganic filler of nanoparticles [17,18]. Moreover, improvements in the luminescent properties can be expected with distribution of rare earth (RE3+) ions doped nanopowders in PCL [6,14]. Luminescent nanoparticles are used in many fields of applications, such as bio-imaging, nano-sensors as well as in light emitting diodes [19,20]. White light emitting diodes (WLED's) have been attracting a great deal of attention in solid state due to their favourable characteristics such as longer lifespan, lower energy consumption, higher reliability and environment-friendly affiliations [21–23].

Nanomaterials are attractive dopants to polymers because of their high surface reactivity, which is attributed to very large surface-to-volume ratio [8,24]. Moreover, they possess intriguing properties associated with quantum confinement effects, making their interaction with different polymers more than attractive [25,26]. ZnO is one of dopants wide and high exciton band gap n-type semiconductor (3.33 eV) at room temperature [19]. Wide band gap materials always present good potential for full colour phosphor. It crystallizes in predominantly hexagonal wurtzite, formed under more general conditions, and cubic zinc blende, which may be formed through possible transformation of cubic blende phase ZnS to ZnO by oxidation [27–29]. Furthermore, ZnO has remarkable performance in electronics, optics, and photonics, which make it attractive for applications such as UV lasers, light emitting diodes, solar cells, nano generators, gas sensors, and photodetectors [30,31].

Studies have been carried out on the effect of nanoparticles on the degradability of electrospun PCL membranes, and the results showed that nanoparticles somehow accelerate the degradation process of PCL nanocomposites [32,33]. However, investigation on properties of solution casting of PCL nanocomposites is still very rare. Lately, the incorporation of RE3+ metal ion doped nanocrystals in polymer nanofibers has been investigated [6,7]. Mainly because polymers doped with RE3+ β-diketonate complexes have attracted considerable interest for they preserve the luminescence properties of the complexes while they can be processed from solution and are mechanically flexible [6,7]. Polymer nanocomposites containing RE3+ ions such as Ce3+ and Eu3+ luminescence centers are useable as large colour areas for studying the properties of the photonic band gap (PBG), which could be important in the development of optical and photo sensor materials [27,34,35].

The current work focuses on the synthesis of the ZnO (undoped, Ce3+, Eu3+ doped and co-doped) nanopowders by the chemical bath deposition (CBD) method and the novel PCL/ZnO: Ce3+, Eu3+ nanocomposites prepared through the solution casting method using acetophenone as solvent. The solution casting is unique because it does not require conventional extrusion or injection molding technologies (which are expensive), yet it readily incorporates components and features traditionally produced by these processes [36–40]. In reality, the current study takes advantage of the solution casting method because the polymer solution would catalyse binding of metal ions, which promotes distribution of metal precursors in the solution and the formation of uniform metal oxide films [41,42]. Some of ideal achievements would be to achieve several goals of combining the properties of ZnO nanopowders and the PCL, which may produce unique material of interest. For example, the interaction between ZnO derivatives and polymer can provide several new radiative recombination mechanisms that may be utilized for broadband light emission or wavelength tunable emission [43,44]. This kind of systems are relevant for optoelectronic and sensor applications [10,11]. Even though PCL is a commercially available plastic, there is still lack of reported work on the use of PCL/phosphor nanocomposites for optics. Moreover, study on photoluminescence (PL) of PCL is rare since most studies concentrate more on the thermal and mechanical properties of the PCL.

All the chemicals used for the preparation of the powders were of analytical grade. These includes: zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O), cerium acetate (Ce(CH3CO2)3·xH2O), thiourea ((NH2)2CS) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH). The Poly (ε-caprolactone) (PCL) and acetophonone (C6H5–CO–CH3) were of a commercial grade with the following specifications: PCL glass transition and melting temperatures are −61 °C and 60 °C respectively; it has the density, 1.14 g cm−3 and the molecular weight (Mw) of 5.0 × 104 g mol−1. Acetophenone (S65 38628) had a density of 1.03 g cm−3 and a boiling point of 202 °C.

During the chemical bath deposition (CBD) of the nanopowders, the precursors were dissolved in deionized water and NH4OH was used as a complexing agent. The amount of each solution was taken in the following order: 60 mL of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O was first placed in the water bath at constant temperature of 80 °C followed by addition of 60 mL of (NH2)2CS and NH4OH was then added dropwise in the mixture with continuous stirring for 10 min. The emulsion was left for 12 h at room temperature. The amount of solutions of zinc acetate, thiourea and ammonia was held constant at a molar ratio of 1:1:1. The ratio of Ce(CH3CO2)3·xH2O): (Eu(CH3CO2)3·xH2O) presented in this study were varied at 1:0, 0:1 and 1:1 mol % Ce3+: Eu3+. The precipitates were then vacuum filtered and washed with 60 mL ethanol, to remove impurities and then left to dry at ambient temperature for 3 days. The nanopowders were then annealed in the furnace at 700 °C in air for 2 h using the furnace L-1200, with model no: RS 1018.

In order to prepare the PCL films, 0.50 g of PCL (-C 6 H 10 O 2-)n were dissolved in 10 mL acetophenone (C6H5–CO–CH3) as a solvent, which was placed in a 45 °C water bath. This step was repeated with the dispersion of 0.025 g of the prepared nanopowders (ZnO, ZnO: 1% Eu3+, ZnO: 1% Ce3+ and ZnO: 1% Eu3+: 1% Ce3+) in the 10 mL PCL/(C6H5–CO–CH3) solution. The polymer mixtures, of the ratio of 95:5 w/w PCL: nanopowders were stirred for an hour and casted in an evaporating dish to coalesce into a nanocomposite for 20 days at room temperature. The phosphor powder and nanocomposite samples were then characterized using different analysis techniques.

The structural analysis of the samples was performed with a Bruker D8 AXS Discover diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα (1.54 Å) radiation with 8-slot sample changer with rotation stage. Continuous scans were carried out at 40 mA and 40 kV using a locked coupled at a count interval of 1 s over a wide range of Bragg's angle (0 ≤ 2θ ≤ 90°). Morphology and chemical composition of the prepared samples (nanopowders and nanocomposites) were analysed using a Tescan Vega3 scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an Oxford X-MaxN energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed on the PerkinElmer DSC 6000. The PCL and nanocomposites sealed in aluminium pans with weight around 5–10 mg were heated from −25 to 120 °C with heating rate of 10 °C.min−1 (first run). The samples were cooled to 25 °C at a cooling rate of 10 °C.min−1 (second run) and reheated up to 120 °C at the same rate. The degree of crystallinity (Xc) of PCL and the nanocomposites was determined using equation (1) [45,46]:

where ΔΗm is the experimental melting enthalpy, ΔΗ°m is the melting enthalpy of 100% crystalline PCL taken as 139.50 J g−1 and wPCL is the weight fraction in the PCL nanocomposites [47,48]. The thermos-gravimetric analysis (TGA) was done on the PerkinElmer TGA. The samples of around 25 mg were heated from 30 to 600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1, under constant nitrogen flow (50 cm3 min−1). Parameters such as reflectance and transmittance were measured with a PerkinElmer UV–vis Lambda 950 spectrophotometer. The optical measurements were carried out in the 200–800 nm range. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) measurements were done at ambient temperature using a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer system, equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source.

The XRD patterns of the nanopowders are shown in Fig. 1 . The diffraction peaks of the nanopowders are located at 30 and 70°, and consistent with the ZnO hexagonal wurtzite crystal structure (JCPDS 75–0576 with lattice parameters a = 3.25 and c = 5.21 Å). There is an impurity noted (marked with *) at around 27.5° for ZnO: 1% Ce3+ singly doped and ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ due to the presence of the Ce2O3 [46,49–51]. The impurity peak, Eu2O3 possible in ZnO: 1% Eu3+ could not be detected [49,52,53].

The XRD patterns of the PCL and nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 2 . The clear and sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ of 21.92°, 21.96° and 24.23°, which correspond to the (110), (111) and (200), are evidence of the semi-crystalline nature of orthorhombic PCL as confirmed by the JCPDS 492111 [17,54–58]. The slightly shifted of the peak position to higher angles than those of the JCPDS 492111 was observed due to processing strain during coalescing and drying of the nanocomposite [59–61]. The XRD patterns of the nanocomposite show the presence of both the PCL and hexagonal wurtzite ZnO structures.

Average crystallite sizes (D) [62–66] listed in Table 1 were estimated by the Debye-Scherrer equation from the crystal planes (100), (002), (101) for the nanopowders and (110), (002) for nanocomposites. The D values of the co-doped nanopowders increases as compared to that of the undoped ZnO and ZnO: 1% Ce3+ doped (see Table 1). Similarly, estimated D of nanocomposites increase as compared to PCL which suggests that powders tend to attract each other within the nanocomposites [67–69,69–72]. In fact, some researchers clearly associated it with the inhomogeneous distribution of agglomerated particles in the nanocomposites [41,73,74]. The Braggs’ interplanar spacing (d) [63,75] for the nanopowders were shown in Table 1. A slight increase in the d of the Eu3+ doped as well as the co-doped nanopowders were observed as depicted in Table 1 compared to the undoped ZnO. Similarly, d spacing of nanocomposites shows a notable increase relative to PCL even though there is fluctuation. The observed increase in d suggests that large radii of dopant ions Ce3+ (1.03) and Eu3+ (0.95) substitute into smaller radius Zn2+ (0.74) with doping and co-doping [7,46]. The substitution also explains the volume increase of D values in this study. It is interesting to note that nanocomposites with large d spacing are narrower to those with small d spacing.

In Fig. 3 the diffraction peaks of (101) for nanopowders and (110) plane for PCL and PCL nanocomposites are shown. There is a slight decrease in the diffraction intensity of the nanopowders in comparison to ZnO as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). That is known to emanate from the degradation of the crystalline quality of the doped and co-doped nanopowders [63,75–77]. However, in this study there is a slight shift to lower angles in Fig. 3 (a). Similar observations were elaborated in the preceding papers that the dopants Eu3+ and Ce3+ ions are well substituted into the Zn2+ ions [41,49]. A slight narrowing in the diffraction peaks of the nanopowders observed in Fig. 3 (a) also explains the slight increase in estimated D shown in Table 1. When compared to PCL, the diffraction peaks (in Fig. 3 (b)) of PCL/ZnO nanocomposites are slightly towards higher angles, while those of PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ doped and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ co-doped are considerably shifted towards smaller 2θ values. The slight diffraction peak shift towards higher angles of the PCL/ZnO nanocomposites implies that there are residual strain and structural deformation due to the influence of PCL matrix and a possible reduction in the d spacing. When the PCL polymer matrix interacts with the surface of ZnO nanopowder, there may be forces exerted, causing tension or compression strains. On the other hand, the shift towards lower angles could suggest a possible increase in the lattice parameters of PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ than those of PCL and PCL/ZnO [8,52]. There is also a notable increase in the peak intensity of the PCL characteristic peak of (110) of these (PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+) nanocomposites than those of PCL and PCL/ZnO. This increase in the diffraction intensity suggests that there is an improvement in the crystalline quality of the PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ nanocomposites. The slight increase in estimated D depicted in Table .1 can further ascertain this increase in the peak intensity. It also suggests good interaction between the PCL matrix and PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ doped and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ co-doped nanopowders [41,77]. However, Malimabe MA et al. [41] observed the opposite which could be an indication that doped and co-doped nanopowders show good interaction with the semi-crystalline polymers like PCL than amorphous polymers such as PVC. Eu3+ containing nanocomposites show a slight narrowing coupled with an increase in the crystalline quality of PCL. There is a slight peak broadening of Ce3+ containing nanocomposites as compared to PCL observed in Fig. 3 (b). This broadening is an indication of a reduction in crystallinity coupled with a slight decrease in the crystalline quality of PCL when filled with the Ce3+ doped nanopowders. PCL chains were restricted by the nanopowder aggregates, such that it is difficult to crystallize, resulting in a decrease in crystallinity for ZnO: 1% Ce3+ nanocomposites [41,50].

The surface morphology of the nanopowders, PCL and nanocomposites was examined using SEM as shown in Figs. 4 and 5
, respectively. For the nanopowders (Fig. 4 (a)–(c)), it was observed that for the undoped ZnO the particles appear as a homogeneous star-like morphology and is agglomerated in the Ce3+, Eu3+ singly doped nanopowders, see Fig. 4 (a)–(c). The ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ co-doped nanopowders in Fig. 4 (d) are more aggregated forming apparent particle growth of the ZnO structures and that morphology is known in literature [49,78,79]. The observation seems to be in line with XRD analysis where there was a clear increase in the D in co-doped nanopowders (see Table .1). The apparent change in morphology coupled with an increase in particle size in the co-doped nanopowders is attributed to the differences of ionic radii of Eu3+, Ce3+ and Zn2+ ions as well as the differences in electronegativity of Eu (1.0), Ce (1.1) and Zn (1.7) [41,80–82].

In Fig. 5 (a) the surface micrographs of PCL show a smooth morphology on larger areas but have irregular and random voids on the surface. Fig. 5 (b) and (c) show agglomerates of the PCL/ZnO and PCL/ZnO: 1% Eu3+ embedded in the PCL matrix. The Ce3+ containing nanocomposites show homogeneous dispersion of the PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+ nanopowders in Fig. 5 (d). The SEM image of PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ nanocomposites presented in Fig. 5 (e) show chunks of aggregated particles on the smooth areas of the PCL surface. During the coalescing of the PCL molecules and the solvent is eliminated, the nanopowders tend to agglomerate as the nanocomposite dries up. They also agglomerate because of the strong particle-particle attraction force that exists between them [41,83]. This might explain the shift of the diffraction peaks of nanocomposites to lower angles in XRD, as well as the red shift in the optical properties [41,59,84].

The elemental composition of PCL and its nanocomposites were detected with an EDS technique as depicted in Fig. 6 (a) – (e). PCL has the molecular formula (C6H10O2)n, thus the EDS spectrum in Fig. 6 (a) suggests the existence of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) at Kα ≈ 0.277 and 0.525 keV respectively. The C energy is also due to the conductive carbon film used for coating during the EDS measurement. The EDS spectra of the nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 6 (b)–(e). The following expected elements composition Zn, Ce, and Eu peaks were observed. There were no other elements detected, which suggest that there were no impurities on the prepared samples [26,41,56].

The thermal analysis of the PCL and nanocomposites were carried out to investigate the effect of the phosphor powder in PCL. The DSC endothermic (heating) and exothermic (cooling) curves of PCL and nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 7 . In Table 2 , the thermal data derived from Fig. 7 are illustrated. PCL endothermic peak at 56.70 °C, shown in Fig. 7 (a), corresponds to the melting of PCL crystals. The melting peak values presented in Fig. 7 (a) and Table 2 are lower than of the as-received PCL pellets. While a decrease in crystallites size could be a possibility, but in this study there seems to be a pronounced influence of plasticization during the dissolution process of the PCL in the acetophenone [32,41,44,85]. Endothermic peaks (Tm) of the nanocomposite are observed at around 56.70 °C with the onset melting temperatures at 57.20 °C on average. The consistency in Tm of the nanocomposites is due to strong particle-particle intermolecular forces between nanopowders in the nanocomposites [86–88]. This observation is in agreement with agglomeration observed in SEM. Interestingly, the melting peaks of PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ shifted slightly to higher temperatures than the rest. The observation is normally associated to the stiffening effect by nanopowders [1,4,89]. However, in this study, the shift could be associated to the formation of large crystallites because XRD confirmed broader peak and high crystalline quality.

There is a slight decrease in the enthalpies of melt (ΔHm) of nanocomposites with some fluctuations from the ΔHm of PCL as depicted in Table 2. The same trend is detected on the degree of crystallinity (Xc) values. In fact, it is known that the addition of nanopowders into polymer matrix could either increase or decrease the Xc of nanocomposites depending, amongst others, on the polymeric matrix type [74,84,90]. In this study, the observed decrease could be attributed to heterogeneous nucleation that leads to the formation of defects ridden crystalline lamella and less ordered crystals of PCL [91,92]. In addition, since PCL is semi-crystalline, the addition of nanopowders increases the amorphous parts in the nanocomposites consequently inhibiting the crystal formation [93,94]. The nanopowders, trapped into the PCL matrix, disrupt the polymer-polymer interaction as the intra chain separation increases. The polymer chains reorganizes thus bringing disorder to the original PCL crystalline structure [95–97].

The exothermic peaks (Tc) of PCL in Fig. 7 (b) appear at 28.04 °C and there is a slight shift to lower temperatures in nanocomposite probably because of the inhomogeneous distribution of the nanopowders in the nanocomposites as observed in the SEM results. When nanopowders aggregate, the size of the clusters may become large enough to hinder nucleation and growth of PCL spherulites [98–100]. Therefore, crystallization depend on the mobility of the polymer chains, thus when nanopowders are incorporated into polymer matrix, they tend to reduce the polymer chain mobility and stiffens in the nanocomposites [47,101]. Similar outcomes are reported in various study on thermal behaviour of PCL based composites [87,102,103].

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) show the thermal stability of PCL and its nanocomposites using TGA and differential DTG. Generally, PCL exhibits a one-step degradation at around 392.5 °C attributed to both the random chain scission and the unzipping from hydroxyl end leading to the formation of ε-caprolactone [47,54,55,104]. Table 3 depicts the thermal degradation temperatures at 10%, 50% and 393% for all materials. Incorporation of ZnO, generally, amounted to lower thermal stability of the nanocomposites compared to PCL. The observation is fairly expected taking into consideration aggregates and increase in D of nanocomposites as confirmed by SEM and XRD. Furthermore, the observation is attributed to the interaction of the O–H group on the ZnO in the nanopowders and the carbonyl group of the PCL, which weakens the polymer-polymer chain interaction [41,95–97]. Some researchers only attributed the observation to accelerated chain scission by ZnO [98,99,105].

The nanocomposites show residues ranging from 4 to 7 w% at 600 °C due to weak ZnO - metal intermolecular forces within the PCL matrix [28,94,105]. This residue increase is probably due to the contribution of the inorganic part (1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ co-doped undoped, 1% Ce3+-, 1% Eu3+-doped, ZnO-nanoparticles) to the total mass, and the mass residue [1,41,106].

Fig. 9 illustrates the UV–Vis NIR absorption measurements at room temperature from 200 to 800 nm. The diffused reflectance spectra of the nanopowders are shown in Fig. 9 (a) and transmittance spectra of PCL and nanocomposites in Fig. 9 (b). In Fig. 9 (a) the estimated absorption edges of the nanopowders is noted around 381 nm, obviously due to the free exciton from the wide band gap ZnO (3.25 eV) [107–109]. There is also a small peak around 495 nm attributed to either singly ionized oxygen vacancies or interstitial zinc ions absorption of the ZnO [110]. There is a slight shift to higher wavelengths in absorption edges of the doped and co-doped ZnO nanopowders as compared to the undoped ZnO. This slight shift is due to the presence of defect state and the disorder brought about by dopants Ce3+ and Eu3+ [31,52,79,94]. In Fig. 9 (b), there are three absorption edges of the PCL estimated around 260, 283 and 323 nm, which were shifted to higher wavelengths by addition of nanopowders into PCL. Apparently the shift of the nanocomposites towards higher wavelengths compared to PCL is an indication of good interaction of PCL with the nanopowders [41,111,112]. There is a decrease in the transmittance for the nanocomposite samples as compared to PCL, which indicates that the nanopowders absorb light [113,114]. The observed absorption edge of PCL is due to the presence of the π-π* carbonyl groups transition and subsequently the optical energy band gap (Eg) of 4.92 eV [16,61,115].

Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the Eg of the nanopowders that were estimated using Kubelka-Munk function [41,116]. The Eg values for the nanopowders are in the range 3.24 to 3.19 eV. There is a slight decrease in the Eg in the ZnO: 1% Eu3+/1% Ce3+ singly doped nanopowders compared to undoped ZnO. In Fig. 10 (b) the Eg of PCL and PCL nanocomposites were estimated using the Stern relation of near-edge absorption [94,117]. The Eg range for PCL and the nanocomposites is from 3.61 to 3.09 eV. The observed decrease is due to the presence of the defect states and the defect disorder due to nanoparticle distributed through the PCL matrix [94]. This decrease in the Eg of the nanocomposites compared to PCL is in agreement with the XRD results that showed an increase in the particle size due to the incorporation of the dopants with a larger ionic radii, into the sites of the smaller Zn2+ ions [52,94].

The PL excitation and emission spectra of the nanopowders shown in Fig. 11 (a) and (b) were discussed in the preceding paper [41]. Fig. 11 (a) depicts broad bands in the UV region from 223 to 395 nm. Those bands are attributed to transitions from valence band to the conduction band within ZnO nanopowders [52,118]. The peaks around 465, 534 and 592 nm for the Eu3+ doped and co-doped samples are respectively assigned to the Eu3+ electronic transitions 7F0
→
5D2, 7F0
→
5D1 and 7F0
→
5D0 [41,49,119–121]. Emission spectra of the nanopowders in Fig. 11 (b) depicts a broad band peaking around 617 with a shoulder at 725 nm. Both these emissions are associated with the ZnO radiative defects particularly from doubly ionized oxygen vacancies [27,49,52,118,122–127]. Ce3+ doped ZnO also displays a broad band peaking at 617 and a shoulder at 752 nm associated to the uncommon 5D(eg) → 7F Ce3+ transition [49,126,128,129]. Compared to undoped ZnO the Eu3+ doped and co-doped samples showed an increase in the emission intensity. This improvement in luminescence intensity may be due to the increase in crystal size as shown in Table 1 [41,118,125,130]. Ce3+ doped nanopowder projected a slight decrease in emission intensity which may be related to concentration quenching effect [41,52,94,118,130].

Fig. 12 (a) and (b) illustrate the PL excitation and emission spectra of PCL and nanocomposites respectively. The normalized emission spectra are shown in Fig. 12 (c). The excitation spectrum shown if Fig. 12 (a) for the PCL shows two peaks at 265 and 310 nm, which may be due to the π → π* and n → π* transition in the PCL [6,16,103,131]. The incorporation of ZnO in PCL shows that the excitation peaks start to emerge around 232, 340 and 390 nm together with the peaks of PCL. The observed behaviour resonates well with the XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 2. The peak around 232 nm become more prominent for PCL/ZnO: 1% Eu3+. The observed excitation peaks are due to ZnO as discussed before. PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+ behaves exactly the same way as PCL/ZnO. The PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ clearly shows the broad excitation band with peaks around 310, 340 and 379 nm and the peaks around 232 and 265 nm disappears. Note also that for the PCL/ZnO: 1% Eu3+ and PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ that the excitation peaks around 390 nm appears only as a shoulder. Disappearance of these peaks could emanate from the PCL absorption of the photons in wavelength range from 232 to 280 nm [132] whereas the increase in luminescence intensity observed could be due to co-doping. Note that the peaks at 465 and 534 nm start to emerge due to Eu3+. The apparent peaks around 465 and 534 nm are due to the Eu3+ electronic transitions 7F0
→
5D2 and 7F0
→
5D1, respectively. Lastly, with the incorporation of ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ in PCL, the excitation intensity improves 10 times than PCL. This increase suggests an improvement in the crystalline quality of the nanocomposites. The observation is in agreement with the XRD analysis as well as the SEM morphology that showed respective grain growth and significant restrictions in the polymer chains. In Fig. 12 (a), the PCL nanocomposites show a common maximum of the F+ centers at 310 nm (at the wavelength more energy is absorbed in nanocomposites). In addition, the excitation of 310 nm seems to be consistent throughout the PCL and its nanocomposites. Thus, it was for these reasons why all the PCL and nanocomposite were excited at this wavelength. Fig. 12 (b) shows the emission spectra of PCL and nanocomposites. The PCL emissions are observed around 485, 545, 592, 617 and 701 nm due to the PCL π-π* (F+) transitions [16,94,103]. PCL/ZnO nanocomposites exhibited asymmetric emission spectra with a broad peak around 617 nm with the shoulders at 545 and 725 nm. This indicates the combined defects of PCL and ZnO radiative from doubly ionized oxygen vacancies. The Ce3+ containing nanocomposites shows the broad peak around 617 nm of much less intensity probably due to the quenching effect of Ce3+ in the nanocomposite [41,52]. The Eu3+ doped nanocomposites have narrow luminescence bands at 592, 617, 654 and 701 nm attributed to the electronic transitions 5D0 → 7F0, 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 →7F2 and 5D0 →7F4 of Eu3+ [41,49,50,93,133]. In addition, to the Eu3+, the emission peak at around 617 nm could be attributed to the defects from ZnO and PCL [77,94]. The normalized emission spectra of the PCL and nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 12 (c). The results clearly show that the emission due to PCL, Eu3+ and ZnO are located at around 545, 654 and 725 nm, respectively [134–136].

Fig. 13 (a) - (c) illustrates the deconvoluted PL emission spectra of asymmetric ZnO, PCL, PCL/ZnO and PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+, respectively. The emission peak around 617 nm appear in all the deconvoluted spectra in Fig. 13 (a)–(d). This suggests that the emission at 617 nm is due to contribution from the ZnO, PCL, and Eu3+. The peak at around 725 nm (∼1.71 eV) emerges due to the defects from ZnO, while that at around 545 nm is due to PCL as shown in Fig. 12 (c). Deconvolution of PCL in Fig. 13 (b) comprises of the peaks at 1.77 (black fit), 2.01 (green fit) with a shoulder at 2.10 (magenta fit), 2.28 (cyan fit), and 2.56 eV (navy fit). Fig. 13 (c) displays peaks around 2.28 (cyan fit) due to PCL, 2.01 (green fit) and 1.78 eV (magenta fit). Lastly, the emission peaks from the deconvolution of PCL/ZnO: 1% Ce3+: 1% Eu3+ in Fig. 13 (d) are around 1.71, 1.77, 1.89, 2.01, 2.10, and 2.56 eV [93,94,137].

Fig. 14 shows the proposed emission mechanisms for the observed emission peaks of the prepared samples. Electrons excited at a 386 for ZnO and 310 nm for PCL and nanocomposites (illustrated at the corresponding Eg ∼3.21 and 4.00 eV respectively) from the valence band (VB) acquire energy and travel to the conduction band (CB). The excited electrons are de-excited via many channels as depicted in Fig. 14 (a)–(d). Based on the defect level emissions illustrated in Fig. 14 (a)–(d), excited electrons are trapped in the CB and released by non-radiative relaxation (NRR), possibly loose some energy and thus return to the VB [49,138–140].

In Fig. 15 , the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) colour chromaticity diagrams display the position of the coordinates and the expected colour of the samples. The CIE chromaticity diagram displayed in Fig. 15 (a) for the nanopowders shows the luminescence colour of the nanopowders is in the orange vertex. Generally, the results suggest that colour coordinates of the 386 nm light source of the nanopowders are close to the edge, therefore, displays a high colour purity [141,142]. The CIE colour chromaticity diagram for PCL and nanocomposites in Fig. 15 (b) illustrates the luminescence colour shift from the red to orange vertex region. Note that the colour coordinates of the light source from 310 nm of the nanocomposites are similarly close to the edge in the CIE colour chromaticity diagram as an attribute to a high colour purity [141,142].

In this work, the novel PCL/phosphor nanocomposites have been prepared through the solution casting method, using acetophenone as the solvent. The XRD patterns of the nanocomposite showed the presence of both the PCL and hexagonal wurtzite ZnO structures. The XRD peaks of PCL/ZnO: Eu3+ doped and PCL/ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ co-doped nanocomposite showed improvement in the crystalline quality and consequently suggests good interaction between the PCL matrix and ZnO: Eu3+ doped and ZnO: Ce3+: Eu3+ co-doped nanopowders. SEM images showed inhomogeneous distribution in nanocomposites. EDS confirmed the presence of expected elements. DSC investigation showed that addition of nanopowders resulted in an increase in the amorphous regions in PCL thus inhibiting the crystal formation. TGA showed a decrease in the thermal stability of the nanocomposites with the approximate 93% weight loss corresponding to weight loss expected in the unzipping from hydroxyl to ε-caprolactone in PCL. UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectroscopy showed red shift in the nanocomposites. Incorporating nanopowders (undoped ZnO, Ce3+- and Eu3+- doped and Ce3+: Eu3+ co-doped ZnO) in PCL matrix, produced enhanced luminescence intensity in the polymer. These results open a new way of applying the PCL in luminescence materials (e.g. the use of PCL in conducting polymers or emitting polymers).
